planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seeds require chilling to break dormancy and will germinate the following spring.

directions: Eastern Blue-eyed Grass thrives in moist to wet meadows, rain gardens, and pond margins where its slender, grass-like foliage blends naturally into turf or sedge communities. Sow seeds in fall directly onto the soil surface without burial, as light aids germination after cold stratification. Pale to mid-blue, six-petaled flowers with bright yellow centers appear from April through June and are especially attractive to small native bees. Once established, plants self-seed freely in moist, fertile spots and require little maintenance beyond occasional division to prevent overcrowding.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Native Plant Database

planting.note: Sow on the soil surface in fall and allow winter cold to provide the stratification needed for spring germination.

directions: Needle-tipped Blue-eyed Grass is one of the smallest and most delicate members of its genus, making it well suited to rock gardens, thin turf, and open woodland edges where it naturalizes without becoming aggressive. Its very slender, flattened stems are topped with tiny blue-violet flowers featuring yellow centers that open in spring and early summer. Surface-sow seeds in fall on moist, sandy or thin soil and leave them undisturbed through winter for natural cold stratification. Plants establish slowly from seed but once settled will self-sow modestly, gradually forming loose, charming colonies.
sources: Flora of North America - Sisyrinchium mucronatum

planting.note: Surface-sow on moist, sandy-acidic soil at or just after last frost; seeds require light for germination and must not be buried.

directions: Slender Marsh Pink is a graceful annual or biennial of wet pine savannas and coastal plain bogs, producing delicate pink, five-petaled flowers marked with a bright yellow star center on wire-thin branching stems from July through September. Grow in full sun with consistently moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil that mimics its native habitat; it tolerates brief periods of standing water. Surface-sow seeds without covering them, as light is required for germination, and keep the seedbed evenly moist until plants are established. Because plants are short-lived, allow some seed to mature and drop in place each season to maintain a self-sustaining colony.
sources: North Carolina State Extension - Native Wetland Plants

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before transplanting in spring; cold-moist stratify for 4-6 weeks before sowing to improve germination rates.

directions: Blue-flowered Eryngo is a standout native perennial prized for its deep blue to violet-blue, thistle-like flower heads surrounded by ornamental spiny blue bracts that appear in late summer and fall. It is native to the wet, acidic flatwoods, savannas, and bog margins of the southeastern United States, and demands moist to wet, sandy or peaty, acidic soil in full sun to perform well. Start seeds with a period of cold-moist stratification to improve germination, and transplant young plants in spring after they have developed a sturdy root system. Plants are slow to establish but long-lived once settled, and their vivid late-season blooms are highly attractive to bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Eryngium integrifolium

planting.note: Start seeds indoors after cold-moist stratification of 4-6 weeks; transplant into permanently moist or wet soil in spring once seedlings are well established.

directions: Water Eryngo is a tall, robust native perennial of coastal plain marshes, wet pine savannas, and stream borders, producing rounded, thimble-like blue-gray flower heads on strongly upright, branching stems in late summer. It develops a deep taproot that makes established plants difficult to transplant, so choose the permanent planting site carefully and provide consistently wet, full-sun conditions from the start. Seeds benefit from a cold-moist stratification period before sowing, and young transplants should be kept well watered during their first season. This species also serves as a larval host plant for the black swallowtail butterfly, adding significant wildlife value to rain gardens and native wetland plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension - Coastal Plain Native Plants
